hero.tagline: Florida's small claims court offers a streamlined process for resolving disputes up to $8,000, with flexible procedures designed for self-representation.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

limits.split_claims_explanation: If your claim exceeds $8,000, you cannot split it into multiple small claims cases. You must either waive the excess amount or file in a higher court.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Florida allows you 5 years to file a claim for breach of a written contract and 4 years for an oral contract. Ensure you have all relevant documents and evidence to support your claim.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $6,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never completed the work.
claims.notes: You have 5 years from the date of breach to file. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend a friend $3,000, which they promised to repay in 6 months, but they failed to do so.
claims.notes: You have 4 years from the date of breach to file. Gather any evidence of the agreement, such as emails or witness statements.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You lent a colleague $5,000 with the understanding it would be repaid in 12 months, but they defaulted.
claims.notes: For written agreements, you have 5 years to file; for oral agreements, 4 years. Provide documentation of the loan and any repayment attempts.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note for $7,500 but failed to make any payments.
claims.notes: You have 5 years from the date of default to file. Present the original promissory note and records of non-payment.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided graphic design services worth $4,500, but the client never paid the invoice.
claims.notes: You have 5 years to file for written agreements. Include the invoice, service agreement, and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $2,000 on their store credit account.
claims.notes: You have 4 years to file. Provide account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: A tenant owes $3,500 in unpaid rent for the past two months.
claims.notes: You have 5 years to file for written lease agreements. Bring the lease agreement and records of unpaid rent.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid for goods worth $1,200 with a check that bounced due to insufficient funds.
claims.notes: You must send a written demand for payment within 30 days. If unpaid, you can sue for the check amount plus damages.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $40 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 3x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer held liable for default
claims.example: You co-signed a $6,000 loan for a friend who defaulted, and now the lender is seeking payment from you.
claims.notes: You have 5 years to file for written agreements. Provide the loan agreement and evidence of default.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Florida provides a 4-year statute of limitations for most consumer disputes, allowing you to seek remedies for defective products, poor services, and misleading practices.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $1,500 refrigerator that stopped working after two months, and the seller refuses to repair or replace it.
claims.notes: You have 4 years to file. Bring the purchase receipt, warranty, and any communication with the seller.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged you $800 but left your home in poor condition, failing to perform as agreed.
claims.notes: You have 4 years to file. Provide the service agreement, payment proof, and photos of the unsatisfactory work.

claims.name: Faulty auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,500 for repairs that did not fix your car's issue, and refuses to refund or redo the work.
claims.notes: You have 4 years to file. Bring repair invoices, payment records, and any expert opinions on the faulty repair.

claims.name: Contractor failed to complete work
claims.example: You paid a contractor $7,000 to renovate your bathroom, but they abandoned the project halfway.
claims.notes: You have 4 years to file. Provide the contract, payment records, and evidence of incomplete work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver goods
claims.example: You ordered and paid $3,000 for furniture that was never delivered, and the vendor is unresponsive.
claims.notes: You have 4 years to file. Bring order confirmations, payment proof, and any correspondence with the vendor.

claims.name: Unauthorized charges after subscription cancellation
claims.example: You canceled a $50/month gym membership, but they continued to charge your credit card for several months.
claims.notes: You have 4 years to file. Provide cancellation notices, bank statements, and any communication with the gym.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $6,500 that broke down within a week, and the seller refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: You have 4 years to file. Bring the sales contract, payment proof, and any mechanic reports on the defect.

claims.name: Misleading advertising leading to financial loss
claims.example: A company advertised a 'lifetime warranty' on a $2,000 product, but refused to honor it when the product failed.
claims.notes: You have 4 years to file. Provide the advertisement, purchase receipt, and any correspondence with the company.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Florida allows 4 years to file claims for property damage, including vehicle collisions and damage caused by others to your property.

claims.name: Damage to your vehicle from a collision
claims.example: Another driver ran a red light and caused $5,000 in damage to your car.
claims.notes: You have 4 years to file. Bring the police report, repair estimates, and any witness statements.

claims.name: Neighbor's tree fell and damaged your property
claims.example: A neighbor's neglected tree fell during a storm, causing $3,500 in damage to your fence and shed.
claims.notes: You have 4 years to file. Provide photos of the damage, repair estimates, and any communication with the neighbor.

claims.name: Tenant caused significant damage to rental property
claims.example: A tenant left your rental unit with $7,000 in damages beyond normal wear and tear.
claims.notes: You have 4 years to file. Bring the lease agreement, photos of the damage, and repair invoices.

claims.name: Neighbor's pet caused damage to your property
claims.example: A neighbor's dog dug under your fence, causing $1,200 in damage.
claims.notes: You have 4 years to file. Provide photos of the damage, repair estimates, and any communication with the neighbor.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left your watch for repair, but the jeweler damaged it, causing $2,500 in loss.
claims.notes: You have 4 years to file. Bring the repair receipt, photos of the damage, and any communication with the jeweler.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Florida provides specific statutes governing landlord-tenant relationships, including security deposits and rental conditions. Ensure you understand your rights and obligations under these laws.

claims.name: Landlord failed to return security deposit
claims.example: After moving out, your landlord did not return your $2,000 security deposit and did not provide an itemized list of deductions.
claims.notes: Landlords must return the deposit within 15 days or provide written notice of deductions within 30 days. Failure to do so may forfeit their right to retain any portion.

claims.name: Landlord did not provide itemized deductions
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $1,500 from your security deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: Landlords must provide an itemized list of deductions within 30 days. Failure to do so may forfeit their right to retain any portion.

claims.name: Rental unit deemed uninhabitable
claims.example: Your apartment had severe mold issues, making it uninhabitable, and the landlord refused to address the problem.
claims.notes: Landlords are required to maintain habitable conditions. You may be entitled to damages or rent abatement.

claims.name: Landlord wrongfully retained tenant's property
claims.example: After eviction, your landlord kept your personal belongings worth $3,000 without legal justification.
claims.notes: Landlords must follow specific procedures for handling abandoned property. Failure to do so may result in liability.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, effectively evicting you without a court order.
claims.notes: Self-help evictions are illegal in Florida. Tenants may sue for damages resulting from such actions.

claims.name: Landlord failed to return prepaid rent
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent upfront, but after moving out early, the landlord did not refund the unused portion.
claims.notes: Landlords must return any unearned rent upon termination of the lease. Failure to do so may result in liability.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant missed three months of rent payments totaling $4,500.
claims.notes: Landlords can sue for unpaid rent and may also pursue eviction proceedings.

claims.name: Tenant repaired and deducted from rent
claims.example: You paid $600 to fix a broken heater after the landlord failed to address it, and deducted the cost from your rent.
claims.notes: Tenants may have the right to repair and deduct, but must follow specific procedures. Consult the statute for details.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wage disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Florida allows you to recover unpaid wages and may award attorney's fees to the prevailing party in such cases.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $2,000 for hours worked but refuses to pay.
claims.notes: You can sue for unpaid wages and may recover attorney's fees if you prevail.
claims.damage_boost: Attorney's fees may be awarded to the prevailing party.

claims.name: Employer withheld final paycheck
claims.example: After resigning, your employer did not provide your final paycheck of $1,500.
claims.notes: You can sue for your final paycheck and may recover attorney's fees if you prevail.
claims.damage_boost: Attorney's fees may be awarded to the prevailing party.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned commissions
claims.example: You earned $3,000 in sales commissions that your employer refuses to pay.
claims.notes: You can sue for unpaid commissions and may recover attorney's fees if you prevail.
claims.damage_boost: Attorney's fees may be awarded to the prevailing party.

claims.name: Employer did not pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 10 hours of overtime but were not compensated at the required rate.
claims.notes: You can sue for unpaid overtime and may recover attorney's fees if you prevail.
claims.damage_boost: Attorney's fees may be awarded to the prevailing party.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Florida allows you to seek compensation for minor injuries, loaned property, and civil theft, with specific statutes governing each.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped and fell in a store, resulting in $5,000 in medical expenses.
claims.notes: You have 4 years to file. Bring medical records, bills, and any incident reports.

claims.name: Failure to return loaned property
claims.example: You lent a friend your $1,200 camera, but they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: You have 4 years to file. Provide evidence of the loan and any communication regarding the return.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: A neighbor took your $2,500 bicycle without permission and refuses to return it.
claims.notes: You must send a written demand for payment 30 days before filing. If unpaid, you can sue for treble damages.
claims.damage_boost: Treble damages (3x the value of the stolen property) plus attorney's fees.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Florida provides avenues to address disputes related to vehicle repairs and wrongful towing, with specific statutes governing each.

claims.name: Dispute over vehicle repair charges
claims.example: A mechanic charged you $2,000 for repairs that did not fix the issue, and refuses to refund or redo the work.
claims.notes: You have 4 years to file. Bring repair invoices, payment records, and any expert opinions on the faulty repair.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, and the towing company charged you $500 for its return.
claims.notes: You have 4 years to file. Provide photos of the parking spot, towing receipts, and any communication with the towing company.

exclusions.explanation: Eviction cases are handled under a separate summary procedure and are not within the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Disputes involving the title to real property exceed the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Defamation cases often involve complex legal issues and damages exceeding small claims limits.
Professional malpractice claims require expert testimony and are beyond the scope of small claims court.
Matters such as divorce, child custody, and probate are handled by specialized divisions of the court.
Claims against the federal government or involving federal law are outside the jurisdiction of state courts.
Class action lawsuits are complex and exceed the procedural scope of small claims court.
Small claims court cannot grant equitable relief such as injunctions or orders for specific performance.
Workers' compensation claims are handled by the Division of Workers' Compensation, not the courts.
Claims against deceased individuals must be filed in probate court against the estate.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not typically awarded in small claims court. However, statutory penalties may apply in certain cases, such as treble damages for civil theft.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $40 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 3x check amount, whichever is greater.
Treble damages (3x the value of the stolen property) plus attorney's fees.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney's fees may be awarded to the prevailing party in certain cases, such as unpaid wage claims and civil theft.

venue_rules.scenario: Where the cause of action occurred
filing_options: County where the event giving rise to the suit occurred

venue_rules.scenario: Where the contract was entered into
filing_options: County where the contract was entered into
County where the property is located

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially causing delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: E-filing availability varies by county. Check with the local clerk's office for specific procedures.

forms.name: Application for Determination of Civil Indigent Status

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and may cover service fees.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees are typically added to the judgment against the loser.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fees and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment.

entries.clock_starts: Date deposit was due to be returned

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the clock when the fraud is discovered or should have been discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention and serves as evidence of your attempt to settle.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: For bad checks, you must send a written demand for payment within 30 days of the check being dishonored.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: No specific pre-filing demand required for wage claims, but it's advisable to request payment in writing before filing.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: For security deposit disputes, tenants must provide a forwarding address in writing to the landlord to facilitate the return of the deposit.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: No specific pre-filing requirements for consumer protection claims, but documenting all communications is beneficial.

forms.name: Statement of Claim
forms.description: Initiates a small claims case by outlining the plaintiff's allegations and the relief sought.

forms.name: Summons/Notice to Appear for Pretrial Conference
forms.description: Notifies the defendant of the lawsuit and the date of the pretrial conference.

forms.name: Final Judgment
forms.description: The court's official decision in the case, outlining the judgment awarded.

forms.name: Satisfaction of Judgment
forms.description: Filed by the plaintiff to indicate that the judgment has been satisfied.

forms.name: Statement of Counterclaim
forms.description: Filed by the defendant to assert a counterclaim against the plaintiff.

forms.name: Application for Determination of Civil Indigent Status
forms.description: Used to request a waiver of court fees based on financial hardship.

forms.name: Notice of Appeal
forms.description: Filed to initiate an appeal of the small claims court's decision.

methods.name: Sheriff personal service
methods.description: The sheriff personally delivers the summons and complaint to the defendant.
cons: May be slower than private service

methods.name: Certified mail
methods.description: The clerk sends the summons and complaint to the defendant via certified mail with return receipt requested.
cons: Defendant may refuse to accept mail

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: A licensed private process server delivers the summons and complaint to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent or an officer of the corporation. For sole proprietors, serve the individual owner.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with Florida's long-arm statute and the laws of the state where the defendant resides.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located after diligent search, service by publication may be permitted.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, you may request the court to allow alternative methods of service.

response_and_default.response_notes: Defendants are not required to file a written answer unless ordered by the court but must appear at the pretrial conference.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to appear at the pretrial conference, the court may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must provide evidence to support the claim before a judgment is entered.

motion_to_vacate_form.name: Motion to Set Aside Default Judgment

response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: If the motion to vacate is denied, the defendant may appeal the decision within 30 days.

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: If a counterclaim exceeds the small claims jurisdictional limit, the case may be transferred to the appropriate court.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may be represented by attorneys, but it is not required.
hearing.format: Bench trial with informal procedures, typically lasting 10-15 minutes per case.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Parties must arrange for their own interpreters if needed.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within a few weeks
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Some counties offer mediation services on-site; check with the local court.

appeals.type_notes: The appeal is a new trial in the circuit court.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment cannot be appealed directly; the defendant must first file a motion to vacate.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of the debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a writ of garnishment with the court, serve the employer, and the employer withholds a portion of the debtor's wages to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor is employed and earns above the exemption threshold.
exemptions: Head of family wages up to $750 per week are exempt

methods.name: Bank account levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from the debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of garnishment, serve it on the debtor's bank, and the bank freezes and turns over funds to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor has sufficient funds in the account.
exemptions: Social Security and other federal benefits are exempt

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on the debtor's real property to secure the judgment.
methods.description: Record a certified copy of the judgment with the county recorder to place a lien on the debtor's property.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns real property; lien must be satisfied before property can be sold or refinanced.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Seize and sell the debtor's non-exempt personal property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, have the sheriff seize the debtor's property, and sell it at auction.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns valuable non-exempt personal property.
exemptions: Personal property up to $1,000 is exempt

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to renew the judgment before the 20-year period expires.
exempt_assets: Social Security and federal benefits — exempt
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy may discharge the judgment; certain debts are non-dischargeable.
collection.priority_notes: Judgment liens are prioritized by the date of recording; earlier liens have higher priority.

businesses.self_representation_notes: A business may be represented by an officer or authorized employee in small claims court.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under a fictitious name must register with the state and may need to provide proof in court.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: A sole proprietor may authorize an employee to represent the business in small claims court.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may be barred from suing to recover fees for unlicensed work.

county_variations.detail: Miami-Dade County offers online filing through the Florida Courts E-Filing Portal.
Broward County requires mandatory mediation at the pretrial conference for claims over $5,000.

pitfalls.title: Filing in the wrong venue
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing in the incorrect county can result in dismissal or transfer, causing delays and additional costs.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Ensure you file in the county where the defendant resides or where the cause of action occurred.

pitfalls.title: Exceeding the monetary limit
pitfalls.what_happens: Claims exceeding $8,000 are outside small claims jurisdiction and may be dismissed.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: If your claim exceeds $8,000, consider waiving the excess or filing in a higher court.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: Failure to serve the defendant correctly can delay the case or lead to dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow Florida's rules for service of process, using authorized methods and ensuring timely service.

pitfalls.title: Missing the statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the statute of limitations expires results in automatic dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Verify the applicable statute of limitations for your claim type and file within the allowed time frame.

pitfalls.title: Not bringing necessary evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Lack of evidence can lead to losing the case, even if your claim is valid.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Bring all relevant documents, receipts, contracts, and witness information to support your claim.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring counterclaims
pitfalls.what_happens: If the defendant files a counterclaim and you don't respond, you could lose by default.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Address any counterclaims promptly and prepare your defense accordingly.

recent_changes.title: Increase in small claims monetary limit
recent_changes.description: The jurisdictional limit for small claims court increased from $5,000 to $8,000, allowing more cases to be heard in this forum.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you are not required to have a lawyer in small claims court. The process is designed for individuals to represent themselves, though you may choose to hire an attorney if you prefer.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: Small claims cases typically resolve within 30 to 60 days from filing to judgment, depending on the court's schedule and complexity of the case.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: The maximum amount you can sue for in Florida small claims court is $8,000, excluding costs, interest, and attorney's fees.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear at the pretrial conference or trial, the court may enter a default judgment in your favor.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal a small claims judgment to the circuit court within 30 days of the judgment date.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment in Florida is valid for 20 years and can be renewed before it expires.

state_specific_notes: Florida allows treble damages for civil theft claims under Fla. Stat. § 772.11.
Landlords must return security deposits within 15 days or provide written notice of deductions within 30 days, per Fla. Stat. § 83.49.
Wage garnishment exemptions include head of family wages up to $750 per week.
Homestead property is generally exempt from forced sale under Florida's constitution.
Florida's small claims court procedures are designed to be accessible for self-represented litigants.

interesting_findings: Florida's increase of the small claims monetary limit to $8,000 in 2020 expanded access to the court for higher-value disputes.
The state's provision for treble damages in civil theft cases provides a strong deterrent against theft.
Florida's homestead exemption offers significant protection for homeowners against creditors.

sources.label: Fla. Stat. § 34.01 (County Court jurisdiction — small claims)
Florida Courts Help — Small Claims Forms
Fla. Stat. Chapter 501 Part II (FDUTPA — Consumer Fraud)
Fla. Stat. § 448.08 (Attorney's fees for unpaid wages)
